summary: AI-native identity operating system for humans, machines, and agents.
editor_note: Identity systems were brittle before every team added agents, service accounts, and shadow SaaS. Oak's official site frames the wedge as a live graph across human, machine, and AI-agent identities, with pages for intelligence, agent governance, and connectors. Compared with static access reviews, the product aims to clean up entitlements from actual app usage. The risk is whether a new platform earns enough connector depth.

product_evidence.claim: The official site describes one platform that sees and governs human, machine, and AI-agent identities from a single live graph.
Oak exposes product pages for an identity intelligence layer, AI-agent governance, universal connectors, white-glove migration, and a demo flow.
The funding source says Oak maps access to application usage and can remove no-longer-needed permissions in real time.

market_context.primary_user: CISOs, identity teams, IT operations, and security engineers governing SaaS, machine, and AI-agent access.
market_context.differentiation: Oak is positioning a live identity graph and agent-governance layer as the control plane, rather than selling another static access-review workflow.
market_context.why_now: AI agents and machine identities are increasing the number of non-human actors with production access faster than legacy IAM review cycles can track.

risks.claim: The falsifiable risk is whether Oak can build connector coverage and remediation trust quickly enough for security teams to let it change live permissions.
risks.basis: VentureDex assessment from the official product pages and seed coverage.

risks.claim: TechCrunch names Accel, CRV, and Greylock as co-leads; Accel is used as the lead investor field to keep the single-lead schema resolvable.
risks.basis: Schema handling note based on the TechCrunch seed coverage.
